1: The speaker — YHWH or the anointed one of chapter 61 — will not be silent until Zion's righteousness (tsedaqah) shines like the dawn and her salvation burns like a torch. The intercessory silence-refused is the inverse of Jerusalem's commanded silence-refused watchmen (v 6): both the divine advocate and the human watchers refuse to rest until Zion is restored. The until-structure frames the entire chapter: the goal is Zion's vindication before all nations (v 2).
2: The nations will see your righteousness (tsedaqah) and all kings your glory (kabod, disp 2). You will be called by a new name that YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) himself will give. The new name signals transformed identity — the same as the renamed cities of 1:26 and the new name of the servant in Isaiah 62:4. A name given by YHWH is a name that reveals what YHWH declares to be true about the bearer. Melek/king (disp 2): all kings see Zion's glory, marking the universal scope of the restoration.
3: Zion will be a crown of beauty in the hand of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) and a royal diadem in the hand of her God (elohim, disp 4). The crown/diadem is not worn by YHWH but held in his hand — Zion is YHWH's prize, the proof of his sovereign beauty, displayed to all. The image reverses the exile's shame: the abandoned city becomes the treasured gem.
4: The old names — Forsaken and Desolate — are replaced by new covenant names: My Delight Is in Her (Hephzibah) and Married (Beulah). YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) will delight in Zion and the land will be married. The marriage metaphor resumes from Isaiah 54:4-6: the widowed city is re-espoused. The name-change is simultaneously an ontological declaration — YHWH's naming constitutes the reality he names.
5: As a young man marries a young woman, your sons will marry you — the sons of Zion become her bridegrooms, the restored community re-inhabiting and re-constituting the city. As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so God (elohim, disp 4) will rejoice over you. The divine joy mirrors the human joy of a wedding — YHWH's restoration of Zion is not grudging or reluctant but celebratory.
6: I have stationed watchmen on your walls who will never be silent day or night. Those who remind YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) of his promises (mazkire YHWH — those who call YHWH to remembrance) are given no rest. The watchmen and the YHWH-reminders form an unbroken intercession. The logic: because YHWH has sworn (v 8), the petitioners press YHWH on the basis of his own oath. This is the prayer-theology of covenant: reminding God of his promises is not impertinence but the right use of covenant relationship.
7: Give him no rest until he establishes Jerusalem and makes her a glory (tehillah) in the earth. The until-structure of v 1 now applies to the intercessors: like the speaker (v 1), the watchmen refuse rest until the goal is achieved. The goal is not merely Zion's safety but her praise-worthiness before the earth.
8: YHWH has sworn by his right hand and by his strong arm — a double oath-formula. Never again will he give grain and wine to enemies as food and drink. The oath grounds the community's confidence: YHWH's oath is as reliable as his own arm, the same arm that brought salvation (Isaiah 51:5, Isaiah 52:10, Isaiah 53:1). The YHWH (disp 4) who swears is the covenant LORD whose oaths are as permanent as the Noah covenant (Isaiah 54:9).
9: Those who harvest will eat and praise YHWH (YHWH, disp 4); those who gather the vintage will drink it in the courts of my holiness (qodesh, disp 2). The harvest and vintage are consumed in sacred space — the restored economy is inseparable from worship. The courts of my holiness (hatsrot qodshi) signals that the temple is rebuilt and functioning, and the produce of the land is offered there as first-fruits before being enjoyed.
10: Pass through the gates; prepare the way for the people; build the highway; clear it of stones; raise a signal banner. The highway-preparation echoes Isaiah 40:3 (prepare in the wilderness a highway for YHWH) and Isaiah 57:14 — but here it is addressed to those within Zion who must open the gates for the returning exiles. The banner (nes) is the rally-signal for the scattered nations (Isaiah 11:10-12). The command is urgent: the return is imminent.
11: YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) has declared to the ends of the earth: Say to daughter Zion, Your salvation is coming. The salvation and its reward come with the returning YHWH — salvation is personified as a figure who brings his recompense. This language anticipates Revelation 22:12 (I am coming, and my reward is with me).
12: They will be called the Holy People (qodesh, disp 2), the Redeemed of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4); Zion will be named Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken. The qodesh/holy name applied to the people is the same holiness-designation given to YHWH's arm (Isaiah 52:10) and his courts (Isaiah 62:9) — the community shares in the holiness of YHWH. The new name reverses the exile-name Forsaken (v 4): the city that was abandoned is now permanently sought.
